name: Salinas de Cabo de Gata — circular from San José — Cabo de Gata-Níjar Natural Park
name_original: Salinas de Cabo de Gata — circular desde San José — Parque Natural del Cabo de Gata-Níjar
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar traverse a unique landscape shaped by volcanic activity, featuring rugged rock formations, ancient lava flows, and dramatic coastal cliffs. The terrain transitions from jagged coastlines with coves and beaches to a semi-desert interior, offering diverse riding experiences. The park also includes the Sierra del Cabo de Gata mountain range and the salt flats of Las Salinas, providing varied backdrops for cycling. Best touring cycling routes around National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar The most popular touring cycling route is Salinas de Cabo de Gata — circular from San José — Cabo de Gata-Níjar Natural Park, a 25.2 miles (40.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 1 minute to complete. This moderate route offers views of the salt flats and coastal scenery near San José. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Transandalus Trail: Agua Amarga Loop, a moderate 11.8 miles (19.0 km) path. This route explores the area around Agua Amarga, combining coastal and inland sections. Local touring cyclists also love the Rodalquilar and Caldera de Majada Redonda Loop, a 16.1 miles (25.9 km) trail leading through volcanic terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 9 minutes. Touring cycling around National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar is defined by volcanic rock formations, quiet coastal roads, and arid semi-desert landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from gentle loops to challenging rides with significant elevation gains. The routes in National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 500 reviews. More than 3,600 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar?
A: There are over 100 touring cycling routes mapped in National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences. This includes 25 easy routes, 43 moderate routes, and 43 difficult routes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Cabo de Gata-Níjar?
A: The terrain in National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar is incredibly diverse due to its volcanic origin. You'll encounter rugged volcanic rock formations, ancient lava flows, and dramatic coastal cliffs. Inland, the landscape transitions to a unique semi-desert environment. Routes often feature quiet coastal roads, but also challenging sections through the Sierra del Cabo de Gata mountain range.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar offers 25 easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for a relaxed ride. Many of these routes allow you to enjoy the scenic coastal views and salt flats without significant elevation challenges. E-bike tours are also a great option to explore gentle climbs and longer distances with ease.

Q: Which routes are suitable for experienced touring cyclists looking for a challenge?
A: For experienced touring cyclists, there are 43 difficult routes available in the park. These often include significant elevation gains and traverse more rugged volcanic terrain. A challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6882748" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabo de Gata Lighthouse – Collado de la Vela Blanca loop from Centro de visitantes Amoladeras&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 55 km with substantial climbing.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1039189417/salinas-de-cabo-de-gata-circular-from-san-jose-cabo-de-gata-nijar-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salinas de Cabo de Gata — circular from San José — Cabo de Gata-Níjar Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, offering views of the salt flats and coastal scenery. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1041915344/transandalus-trail-agua-amarga-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Transandalus Trail: Agua Amarga Loop&lt;/a&gt;, exploring the area around Agua Amarga.

Q: What are some notable attractions or landmarks I can see along the cycling routes?
A: The park is rich in natural beauty and historical sites. Along your rides, you might encounter stunning viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3291513" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views from the Granatilla viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, iconic beaches such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/125186" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Genoveses Beach&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/125188" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mónsul Beach&lt;/a&gt;, and the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1065889" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sirens' Reef&lt;/a&gt;. Historical sites include various castles like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/678878" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Felipe Castle&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2700912" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Ramón Castle&lt;/a&gt;, as well as the abandoned Cortijo del Fraile, known for its literary and cinematic history.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Cabo de Gata-Níjar?
A: The pleasant climatic conditions make Cabo de Gata-Níjar suitable for cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for touring cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer. Winter can also be a good option, with mild temperatures and fewer crowds, allowing for enjoyable rides.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through charming villages or offer places to stop for refreshments?
A: Many routes in the park connect or pass near picturesque whitewashed fishing villages such as San José, Agua Amarga, Isleta del Moro, and Las Negras. These villages offer excellent opportunities to stop for relaxation, enjoy local cuisine, and find refreshments. San José, being the largest village, provides numerous options.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar?
A: The touring cycling routes in National Park Cabo de Gata-Níjar are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 500 reviews. Reviewers often praise the unique volcanic landscapes, the stunning coastal views, and the variety of routes catering to different fitness levels. Many appreciate the wild and isolated character of the park, largely untouched by mass tourism.

Q: Can I find routes that explore the famous salt flats of Las Salinas?
A: Yes, the salt flats of Las Salinas de Cabo de Gata are a significant natural feature and a Ramsar site, crucial for migratory birds like flamingos. You can explore this area on dedicated cycling trails that are closed to cars. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1039189417/salinas-de-cabo-de-gata-circular-from-san-jose-cabo-de-gata-nijar-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salinas de Cabo de Gata — circular from San José — Cabo de Gata-Níjar Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; route is an excellent option to experience this unique wetland environment.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Cabo de Gata lighthouse?
A: Absolutely. The Cabo de Gata lighthouse, built on the ruins of the Castle of San Francisco de Paula, offers breathtaking panoramic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6882748" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabo de Gata Lighthouse – Collado de la Vela Blanca loop from Centro de visitantes Amoladeras&lt;/a&gt; is a route that specifically features this iconic landmark, allowing you to cycle to and enjoy the views from this historic point.

Q: What are the options for parking and access points for touring cyclists?
A: Access to the park is generally good, with various villages like San José, Agua Amarga, and Rodalquilar serving as common starting points. These villages typically offer parking facilities. Many routes are designed as loops, making it convenient to park your vehicle and begin your ride directly from these locations.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through the volcanic terrain of Rodalquilar?
A: Yes, the Rodalquilar area is known for its distinctive volcanic terrain and historical mining past. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1039226890/rodalquilar-and-caldera-de-majada-redonda-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rodalquilar and Caldera de Majada Redonda Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a route that specifically leads through this unique volcanic landscape, offering insights into the geological history of the park. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1041874843/rodalquilar-and-cortijo-del-fraile-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rodalquilar and Cortijo del Fraile Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which also explores this area.
tips: Cabo de Gata is the southernmost town in the park and the closest to the capital, in fact this neighborhood belongs to the municipality of Almería. On its beach we can see the fishermen's boats that have gone out to fish at night.

This tower was built in the 18th century to protect the salt pans on the ruins of a previous one that was part of the network of surveillance towers of Cabo de Gata from the Andalusian period. Despite being an Asset of Cultural Interest, it is in a poor state of conservation awaiting a restoration that is yet to come.

In the salt flats of Cabo de Gata, more than 100 different species of birds that inhabit these wetlands have been recorded. These salt flats are also a resting place in the migratory process of many birds that you can see as you pass through this window.

an 18th century church, was originally built for the salt workers and remains a simple but charming place of worship. Near the church is a striking bronze statue of a man carrying a basket, a tribute to the Ibizan salt workers, made by the sculptor Pedro Hormigo.

Unfortunately, despite intensive requests and suggestions over the last 15 years, the local authority has not managed to create sensible, safe (or at all:) cycle paths along the main routes (except for San Isidro - Campohermoso). There is proven to be a great need for them. Examples are the ubiquitous Hivernadores field workers from the Maghreb and sub-Saharan region (who do the dirty work for rich Northern Europe here for starvation wages in sweltering heat so that we have crisp tomatoes on our plates all year round), who travel on wobbly e-scooters or often on old bikes without lights. Many local operators of gentle tourism have also been pushing for a change of thinking for a long time.
But unfortunately it is still the same as it was 15 years ago (when I was here for the first time). As a cyclist, you are forced to share the unlit asphalt road with motorized traffic. Hardly anyone sticks to the 50 km/h speed limit. Despite (naively and well-intentioned) signs warning that cyclists are on the road, motorcyclists with open silencers sometimes race past you at what feels like 120 km/h in what feels like 1m. Unfortunately: life-threatening.
Built in 1863, the Gata Lighthouse in Cabo de Gata is 18 metres high and flashes every 30 seconds at a height of more than 50 metres above sea level, where it can be observed from 30 miles away.
Located on a beautiful point and a tourist attraction, there are 3 buses of tourists today. The road ends at the top, you can only continue upwards on foot or by bicycle.
The volcanic rocks offer a variety of colors.
One of the most beautiful passages of the Cabo de Gata, truly magnificent.

This mill, built in the 19th century and restored a few years ago, consists of 3 main parts. On one side we have the tower, on top of that the spire and finally the mechanical tree inside. The tower of this mill is notable for its conical shape, going from a wide base to narrower as it grows taller.
